summary: Replaced the old Bash `tools/issues.sh` clang diagnostic scraper with a Rust Cargo workspace tool at `tools/issues`. The new tool uses libclang through `clang-sys`, reads `compile_flags.txt`, scans `src/**/*.[ch]`, collects diagnostics from translation units, deduplicates them with a `HashSet`, sorts them deterministically, and prints grouped output by severity, diagnostic category, warning option, file, and source location. The PR also added Cargo/rustfmt configuration, a lockfile, ignores for Rust/Windows build artifacts, and an AGPL license for the new tool.

key_files.role: Deleted legacy Bash script that iterated over `src` C/H files and grepped clang warning/error lines.
New Rust implementation using libclang APIs to parse translation units, collect diagnostics, deduplicate, sort, and print grouped issue reports.
Defines the `melee-issues` Rust package, Rust 2021 edition, AGPL-3.0-or-later license, and dependencies including `clang-sys`, `glob`, `itertools`, `string-interner`, `anyhow`, and logging crates.
Adds the GNU Affero General Public License v3 text for the new Rust tool.
Adds a repository-level Cargo workspace with `tools/issues` as a member.
Pins Cargo dependencies for reproducible builds of the tooling.

what_changed: The previous shell script invoked `clang @compile_flags.txt -fno-color-diagnostics -fsyntax-only -ferror-limit=0` for every `.c` and `.h` file and filtered stderr with grep.
The replacement Rust tool changes directory to the project root using `CARGO_MANIFEST_DIR`, reads `compile_flags.txt`, appends `-ferror-limit=0`, and passes those flags to libclang.
The tool scans source files via the glob pattern `src/**/*.[ch]`.

Diagnostics are extracted through libclang calls such as `clang_parseTranslationUnit2`, `clang_getDiagnosticSetFromTU`, `clang_getDiagnosticLocation`, `clang_getDiagnosticSeverity`, `clang_getDiagnosticCategoryText`, `clang_getDiagnosticOption`, and `clang_getDiagnosticSpelling`.
Diagnostic strings are interned with `string-interner`, and complete diagnostic records are stored in a `HashSet<DiagnosticInfo>` for deduplication.
Output is sorted by severity, category ID, option string, path depth/lexicographic path, line, column, and spelling.
Output is grouped hierarchically with counts at each level: severity, category, option or `required`, filename, then line/column/spelling.
The command described by the PR body is `cargo run -r tools/issues`; requirements include Cargo and dynamically linked libclang.
smart_moves: Using libclang directly avoids brittle stderr text parsing and makes category, option, severity, location, and spelling available as structured data.
Hashing full `DiagnosticInfo` records gives a simple deduplication mechanism before sorting and printing.
The deterministic sort order makes issue output stable and easier to review across decompilation changes.
Grouping diagnostics by severity, category, warning option, and file turns a flat warning stream into a navigable backlog for cleanup work.
Interning repeated strings reduces duplicated storage for filenames, diagnostic categories, options, and diagnostic messages across many source files.
Adding Cargo workspace metadata and a checked-in lockfile makes the tool more reproducible than an ad hoc script.
decomp_lessons: For repository-wide C diagnostic tracking, prefer structured compiler APIs over grepping compiler output; it makes sorting, grouping, deduplication, and future automation more reliable.

A diagnostic report grouped by warning option is useful for decomp projects because it exposes classes of cleanup work such as typedef redefinitions, incompatible pointer types, missing prototypes, return-type problems, and uninitialized variables.
Stable issue ordering matters: when tool output is deterministic, future PRs can compare warning-count changes without noise from file traversal order.
A tool that reads the same `compile_flags.txt` used by the project keeps diagnostics aligned with the real decompilation compile environment.
When introducing Rust tooling into a C decomp repository, include workspace setup, lockfile, formatting config, artifact paths, and gitignore updates in the same PR.
naming_conventions: `melee-issues` is used as the Cargo package name for the rewritten issues tool.
The Rust code names the core diagnostic record `DiagnosticInfo`.
The severity enum mirrors libclang diagnostic severities with variants `Ignored`, `Note`, `Warning`, `Error`, and `Fatal`.
Diagnostic output labels diagnostics without a clang option as `required`.
The new tool lives under `tools/issues/`, replacing the single-file `tools/issues.sh` script.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: No direct assembly matching tactic is present in this PR.
The indirect decompilation tactic is to improve C/header cleanup by producing a structured diagnostic backlog from all `src/**/*.c` and `src/**/*.h` files.
review_feedback: No review comments or issue comments were present in the provided PR slice.
The PR body explicitly states the motivation: the tool was rewritten in Rust and entries are sorted, grouped, and deduplicated.
The PR body documents external setup requirements for libclang on Fedora, Ubuntu/WSL, Arch, NixOS, and Windows, including possible `LIBCLANG_PATH` configuration on Windows.
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that modify `tools/issues/src/main.rs` or change diagnostic grouping behavior.
Search for PRs that reduce warning categories shown by `melee-issues`, especially incompatible pointer types, typedef redefinitions, return-type warnings, and uninitialized-variable warnings.
Check whether CI or developer documentation later added `cargo run -r tools/issues` as a standard health check.
Look for later changes to `compile_flags.txt` that affected the output of the issues tool.
